details.request: Bitcoin account controlled by either one of two keys: a preferred one that is used most often (90% of the time) and an alternate one that is used in specific circumstances (10% of the time) or as backup
A married couple wants to have a joint Bitcoin account for their day-to-day expenses which either of them can spend from.

Parents want to set up an account for their teenage child. For now, the child can spend the funds with confirmation from either parent. When he turns 21 on November 4 2028, he can spend the funds on his own.
A user and a 2FA service need to sign off, but after 90 days the user alone is enough so the funds don't get stuck if the 3rd party service becomes unavailable.
A technology company has a special fund for R&D projects managed by the CTO under the control of the CEO and CFO. The CTO needs the confirmation from either the CEO or CFO to spend the funds.

A hacker is selling valuable data to an anonymous buyer. The data is contained in an encrypted file. Through zero-knowledge proofs, the hacker can cryptographically prove that the data is genuine and the decryption key hashes to a certain value H without revealing the key itself. Since there is no trust relation between buyer and seller, they use an HTLC scheme to proceed with the trade. The buyer sends the agreed bitcoin amount to the seller but uses the decryption key hash to make sure the seller discloses the decryption key when claiming the funds. If the seller doesn't claim the funds within an hour, the buyer is able to reclaim the funds.

In the event of my death, the executor of my will has the private key to my wallet and will pass it on to my children. As a security measure, the key only moves the funds together with a passphrase that has been communicated separately to my children.
A council with 11 participants that can spend the funds if a majority reaches an agreement and only after 40 days.

details.predicted:
pk Alice
after 90d pk Bob (Bob being an identifier for the third-party signing authority)
or older(180d) pk Alice (Alice's key must be at least two years old to sign off alone after this period)

Note: In a real-world scenario, you would need actual public keys or identifiers instead of names like "Alice" and "Bob". The above policy assumes that Bob is the third-party service with its own signing authority. If Alice has her private key for 2FA signed by an external party (like Google Authenticator), then after 90 days without a new signature from this entity, she can sign off alone provided she's been using it continuously and meets any additional requirements set forth in your actual policy document or system rules.

Field name: Policy
Value:
pk Alice
after 90d pk Bob
or older(180d) pk Alice

Parents want to set up an account for their teenage child. For now, the child can spend the funds with confirmation from either parent. When he turns 21 on November 4 2028, he can spend the funds on his own.
A user and a 2FA service need to sign off, but after 90 days the user alone is enough so the funds don't get stuck if the 3rd party service becomes unavailable.

details.request: A technology company has a special fund for R&D projects managed by the CTO under the control of the CEO and CFO. The CTO needs the confirmation from either the CEO or CFO to spend the funds.

A hacker is selling valuable data to an anonymous buyer. The data is contained in an encrypted file. Through zero-knowledge proofs, the hacker can cryptographically prove that the data is genuine and the decryption key hashes to a certain value H without revealing the key itself. Since there is no trust relation between buyer and seller, they use an HTLC scheme to proceed with the trade. The buyer sends the agreed bitcoin amount to the seller but uses the decryption key hash to make sure the seller discloses the decryption key when claiming the funds. If the seller doesn't claim the funds within an hour, the buyer is able to reclaim the funds.
details.predicted:
or(thresh(1,H),pk(H)) 

Explanation:
- `H` represents a pre-agreed upon hash value that both parties know but does not reveal any information about it directly.

This policy ensures:

1. The seller can claim H bitcoins if they disclose their decryption key (via zero-knowledge proof).
2. If the buyer doesn't receive confirmation of disclosure within an hour, he/she is able to reclaim his/her funds by using `H` as a condition for refunding.
3. No trust relation between parties since it relies on cryptographic proofs and HTLC mechanisms.

Note: This policy assumes that H has been agreed upon beforehand in some secure manner (e.g., through another zero-knowledge proof).

details.request: In the event of my death, the executor of my will has the private key to my wallet and will pass it on to my children. As a security measure, the key only moves the funds together with a passphrase that has been communicated separately to my children.
details.predicted:
or(pk(executor),pk(child_1) + hash160(passphrase_child_1)) OR pk(child_2) + hash160(passphrase_child_2) OR pk(child_3) + hash160(passphrase_child_3)
Note: This policy assumes that the passphrases are communicated separately and securely to each child. The `hash160` function is used for hashing a 32-byte input (the concatenation of private key and passphrase).
